title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Remote Work on Corporate Culture

texts.A: The shift to remote work has permanently severed the vital ties that bind a workforce together. [37]Spontaneous collaboration, the lifeblood of innovation, cannot be replicated on a video call.[/37] When employees are isolated, their connection to the company’s core mission withers. [38]While some tout the benefits of flexibility, the long-term cost is a fragmented culture and a loss of institutional knowledge.[/38] [40]Mentorship, in particular, has suffered; junior staff no longer benefit from the 'osmosis' of watching senior leaders navigate complex challenges in real-time.[/40]
texts.B: The traditional office was never the crucible of culture we imagine it to be; for many, it was a site of performative productivity and unnecessary stress. [40]Remote work has democratized the professional sphere, allowing merit to take precedence over physical presence.[/40] The 'loss of innovation' argument is a fallacy pushed by those who fear a loss of control. [37]With the right digital tools, global teams can collaborate more effectively than those limited by a shared physical zip code.[/37] The future is asynchronous and output-focused.
texts.C: We are currently in a messy transition phase, but the 'hybrid' model is the only sustainable path forward. [39]Total remote work leads to the isolation described by critics, but a total return to the office is an affront to the work-life balance employees now rightfully demand.[/39] The key is intentionality. Offices should be redesigned as 'hubs' for specific collaborative events, while deep, focused work happens at home. Those who insist on one extreme or the other are ignoring the nuanced reality of modern labor psychology.
texts.D: Arguments against remote work often mask a deep-seated lack of trust in employees. However, we must acknowledge that humans are social animals. [39]The lack of physical proximity leads to a subtle but measurable decline in 'social capital'—the trust and empathy built during coffee breaks and hallway chats.[/39] Without this capital, conflict resolution becomes harder. [38]While productivity might remain high in the short term, the social fabric of the organization eventually thins, making it harder to retain talent during turbulent times.[/38]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B regarding the impact of physical presence on collaboration?
questions.explanation: B argues that digital tools allow global teams to work better than those in the same zip code. A argues that spontaneous collaboration 'cannot be replicated' without being physically together.

questions.question: Which expert shares D's concern about the social consequences of long-term remote work?
questions.explanation: D mentions a decline in 'social capital' and 'social fabric.' A similarly notes that the 'vital ties' are severed and the workforce becomes 'fragmented.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar opinion to C about the flaws of a 'pure' remote work model?
questions.explanation: C states that 'total remote work leads to isolation.' D agrees that 'lack of physical proximity' causes a decline in trust and social connection.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the effect of remote work on junior employees' development?
questions.explanation: A claims mentorship has suffered because junior staff can't learn by osmosis. B argues that remote work 'democratizes' the sphere and allows 'merit to take precedence,' implying a more fair environment for all to succeed.
